The combined shrine was renamed "Katsushika Shrine" and elevated to the status of a [[village shrine]] (村社).

== Grounds ==  
Though the shrine grounds are modest at 24 tsubo, it is surrounded by several notable {{ill|black pine|en|black pine|WD=Q4921461|lt=black pine|1=Black pine}} (クロマツ) trees, designated as one of the "Ten Famous Trees of Funabashi." The shrine is located along [[:en:National Route 14|National Route 14]], a route historically known as the [[Narita Highway]], frequented by pilgrims to [[Naritasan Shinshoji Temple]] and travelers along the [[Sakura Highway]].<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.midori-kikin.or.jp/tree/main4.html|title=Katsushika Shrine Black Pine Trees|publisher=Funabashi City Green Fund|accessdate=2009-04-12}}</ref>

== Access ==  
The shrine is accessible from:  
* {{ill|East Japan Railway Company|en|East Japan Railway Company|WD=Q499071|lt=East Japan Railway Company|1=East Japan Railway Company}}, {{ill|Tokyo Metro|en|Tokyo Metro|WD=Q682894|lt=Tokyo Metro|1=Tokyo Metro}}, or [[:en:Tōyō Rapid Railway|Tōyō Rapid Railway]] {{ill|Nishi-Funabashi Station|en|Nishi-Funabashi Station|WD=Q128803|lt=Nishi-Funabashi Station|1=Nishi-Funabashi Station}}

[[Katsushika Shrine]] is a regional [[Sōja shrine|Sōja shrine]]. It enshrines many kami of the local region.

It contrasts with provincial Soja shrines which enshrine all the kami of a province<ref>https://archive.ph/wip/XtZfZ</ref>

'''Katsushika Shrine''' (葛飾神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|Funabashi|en|Funabashi|WD=Q271417|lt=Funabashi|1=Funabashi}}, {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|en|Chiba Prefecture|WD=Q80011|lt=Chiba Prefecture|1=Chiba Prefecture}}, Japan.

== Enshrined Deities ==  
The shrine is dedicated to:  
* [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]] (瓊瓊杵尊)  
* [[Hikohohodemi-no-Mikoto]] (彦火火出見尊)  
* {{ill|Kumano Okami|ja|熊野大神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Okami|qq=}} (熊野大神)

== History ==  
The name "Katsushika" is commonly found in place names across Japan and refers to fertile, beautiful land covered in vines ("kudzu"). The historical text ''[[Wamyō Ruijushō]]'' mentions the [[Katsushika District]] and the Kurihara area.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.city.funabashi.chiba.jp/k-seibu/HTML/nakayama/ogurihara002.htm|title=The History of Kurihara|publisher=Funabashi City|accessdate=2009-04-13}}</ref><ref>[https://adeac.jp/funabashi-digital-museum/top/ Funabashi Digital Museum - Kurihara]</ref>  

The shrine originally stood near Katsumata Pond (now [[Katsumata Park]]) under the name "Kumano Shrine," which enshrined {{ill|Kumano Okami|ja|熊野大神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Okami|qq=}}. Nearby, a smaller shrine known as "Katsushika Jinja Motomiya" (Katsushika Great Deity) was located near what is now Katsushika Junior High School. A nearby spring called "Kazura no I" (葛羅の井) served as a sacred water source for the shrine.  

The shrine's torii from the ''Bunka'' era (1804–1818) bears an inscription stating that it was the "Sōja" (general shrine) for Katsushika District. This legacy was inherited by the current Katsushika Shrine, which was officially established in {{ill|Taishō era|en|Taishō era|WD=Q329174|lt=Taishō era|1=Taishō era}} 5 (1916) when the smaller shrine was merged with Kumano Shrine. The combined shrine was renamed "Katsushika Shrine" and elevated to the status of a [[village shrine]] (村社).

== Grounds ==  
Though the shrine grounds are modest at 24 tsubo, it is surrounded by several notable {{ill|black pine|en|black pine|WD=Q4921461|lt=black pine|1=Black pine}} (クロマツ) trees, designated as one of the "Ten Famous Trees of Funabashi." The shrine is located along [[:en:National Route 14|National Route 14]], a route historically known as the [[Narita Highway]], frequented by pilgrims to [[Naritasan Shinshoji Temple]] and travelers along the [[Sakura Highway]].<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.midori-kikin.or.jp/tree/main4.html|title=Katsushika Shrine Black Pine Trees|publisher=Funabashi City Green Fund|accessdate=2009-04-12}}</ref>

== Access ==  
The shrine is accessible from:  
* {{ill|East Japan Railway Company|en|East Japan Railway Company|WD=Q499071|lt=East Japan Railway Company|1=East Japan Railway Company}}, {{ill|Tokyo Metro|en|Tokyo Metro|WD=Q682894|lt=Tokyo Metro|1=Tokyo Metro}}, or [[:en:Tōyō Rapid Railway|Tōyō Rapid Railway]] {{ill|Nishi-Funabashi Station|en|Nishi-Funabashi Station|WD=Q128803|lt=Nishi-Funabashi Station|1=Nishi-Funabashi Station}}  
* {{ill|Keisei Electric Railway|en|Keisei Electric Railway|WD=Q83538|lt=Keisei Electric Railway|1=Keisei Electric Railway}} {{ill|Keisei-Nishifuna Station|en|Keisei-Nishifuna Station|WD=Q6383831|lt=Keisei-Nishifuna Station|1=Keisei-Nishifuna Station}}

{{Infobox religious building
| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]
| deity                 = deities of its local area
| type                  = [[Sōja shrine|Sōja shrine]]
| image= File:Katsushika Jinja Shrine Funabashi Chiba.JPG
| prefecture= {{ill|Funabashi|WD=Q271417|1=Funabashi}}
| coordinates= {{Coord|35.710833|N|139.956944|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}
}}
[[Katsushika Shrine]] is a regional [[Sōja shrine|Sōja shrine]]. It enshrines many kami of the local region.

It contrasts with provincial Soja shrines which enshrine all the kami of a province<ref>https://archive.ph/wip/XtZfZ</ref>

'''Katsushika Shrine''' (葛飾神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|Funabashi|en|Funabashi|WD=Q271417|lt=Funabashi|1=Funabashi}}, {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|en|Chiba Prefecture|WD=Q80011|lt=Chiba Prefecture|1=Chiba Prefecture}}, Japan.

== Enshrined Deities ==  
The shrine is dedicated to:  
* [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]] (瓊瓊杵尊)  
* [[Hikohohodemi-no-Mikoto]] (彦火火出見尊)  
* {{ill|Kumano Okami|ja|熊野大神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Okami|qq=}} (熊野大神)

== History ==  
The name "Katsushika" is commonly found in place names across Japan and refers to fertile, beautiful land covered in vines ("kudzu"). The historical text ''[[Wamyō Ruijushō]]'' mentions the [[Katsushika District]] and the Kurihara area.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.city.funabashi.chiba.jp/k-seibu/HTML/nakayama/ogurihara002.htm|title=The History of Kurihara|publisher=Funabashi City|accessdate=2009-04-13}}</ref><ref>[https://adeac.jp/funabashi-digital-museum/top/ Funabashi Digital Museum - Kurihara]</ref>  

The shrine originally stood near Katsumata Pond (now [[Katsumata Park]]) under the name "Kumano Shrine," which enshrined {{ill|Kumano Okami|ja|熊野大神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Okami|qq=}}. Nearby, a smaller shrine known as "Katsushika Jinja Motomiya" (Katsushika Great Deity) was located near what is now Katsushika Junior High School. A nearby spring called "Kazura no I" (葛羅の井) served as a sacred water source for the shrine.  

The shrine's torii from the ''Bunka'' era (1804–1818) bears an inscription stating that it was the "Sōja" (general shrine) for Katsushika District. This legacy was inherited by the current Katsushika Shrine, which was officially established in {{ill|Taishō era|en|Taishō era|WD=Q329174|lt=Taishō era|1=Taishō era}} 5 (1916) when the smaller shrine was merged with Kumano Shrine. The combined shrine was renamed "Katsushika Shrine" and elevated to the status of a [[village shrine]] (村社).

== Grounds ==  
Though the shrine grounds are modest at 24 tsubo, it is surrounded by several notable {{ill|black pine|en|black pine|WD=Q4921461|lt=black pine|1=Black pine}} (クロマツ) trees, designated as one of the "Ten Famous Trees of Funabashi." The shrine is located along [[:en:National Route 14|National Route 14]], a route historically known as the [[Narita Highway]], frequented by pilgrims to [[Naritasan Shinshoji Temple]] and travelers along the [[Sakura Highway]].<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.midori-kikin.or.jp/tree/main4.html|title=Katsushika Shrine Black Pine Trees|publisher=Funabashi City Green Fund|accessdate=2009-04-12}}</ref>

== Access ==  
The shrine is accessible from:  
* {{ill|East Japan Railway Company|en|East Japan Railway Company|WD=Q499071|lt=East Japan Railway Company|1=East Japan Railway Company}}, {{ill|Tokyo Metro|en|Tokyo Metro|WD=Q682894|lt=Tokyo Metro|1=Tokyo Metro}}, or [[:en:Tōyō Rapid Railway|Tōyō Rapid Railway]] {{ill|Nishi-Funabashi Station|en|Nishi-Funabashi Station|WD=Q128803|lt=Nishi-Funabashi Station|1=Nishi-Funabashi Station}}  
* {{ill|Keisei Electric Railway|en|Keisei Electric Railway|WD=Q83538|lt=Keisei Electric Railway|1=Keisei Electric Railway}} {{ill|Keisei-Nishifuna Station|en|Keisei-Nishifuna Station|WD=Q6383831|lt=Keisei-Nishifuna Station|1=Keisei-Nishifuna Station}}

[[Katsushika Shrine]] is a regional [[Sōja shrine|Sōja shrine]]. It enshrines many kami of the local region.

It contrasts with provincial Soja shrines which enshrine all the kami of a province<ref>https://archive.ph/wip/XtZfZ</ref>

'''Katsushika Shrine''' (葛飾神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|qid=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|Funabashi|en|Funabashi|qid=Q271417|lt=Funabashi|1=Funabashi}}, {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|en|Chiba Prefecture|qid=Q80011|lt=Chiba Prefecture|1=Chiba Prefecture}}, Japan.

== Enshrined Deities ==  
The shrine is dedicated to:  
* [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]] (瓊瓊杵尊)  
* [[Hikohohodemi-no-Mikoto]] (彦火火出見尊)  
* {{ill|Kumano Okami|ja|熊野大神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Okami|qq=}} (熊野大神)

== History ==  
The name "Katsushika" is commonly found in place names across Japan and refers to fertile, beautiful land covered in vines ("kudzu"). The historical text ''[[Wamyō Ruijushō]]'' mentions the [[Katsushika District]] and the Kurihara area.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.city.funabashi.chiba.jp/k-seibu/HTML/nakayama/ogurihara002.htm|title=The History of Kurihara|publisher=Funabashi City|accessdate=2009-04-13}}</ref><ref>[https://adeac.jp/funabashi-digital-museum/top/ Funabashi Digital Museum - Kurihara]</ref>  

The shrine originally stood near Katsumata Pond (now [[Katsumata Park]]) under the name "Kumano Shrine," which enshrined {{ill|Kumano Okami|ja|熊野大神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Okami|qq=}}. Nearby, a smaller shrine known as "Katsushika Jinja Motomiya" (Katsushika Great Deity) was located near what is now Katsushika Junior High School. A nearby spring called "Kazura no I" (葛羅の井) served as a sacred water source for the shrine.  

The shrine's torii from the ''Bunka'' era (1804–1818) bears an inscription stating that it was the "Sōja" (general shrine) for Katsushika District. This legacy was inherited by the current Katsushika Shrine, which was officially established in {{ill|Taishō era|en|Taishō era|qid=Q329174|lt=Taishō era|1=Taishō era}} 5 (1916) when the smaller shrine was merged with Kumano Shrine. The combined shrine was renamed "Katsushika Shrine" and elevated to the status of a [[village shrine]] (村社).

== Grounds ==  
Though the shrine grounds are modest at 24 tsubo, it is surrounded by several notable {{ill|black pine|en|black pine|qid=Q4921461|lt=black pine|1=Black pine}} (クロマツ) trees, designated as one of the "Ten Famous Trees of Funabashi." The shrine is located along [[:en:National Route 14|National Route 14]], a route historically known as the [[Narita Highway]], frequented by pilgrims to [[Naritasan Shinshoji Temple]] and travelers along the [[Sakura Highway]].<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.midori-kikin.or.jp/tree/main4.html|title=Katsushika Shrine Black Pine Trees|publisher=Funabashi City Green Fund|accessdate=2009-04-12}}</ref>

== Access ==  
The shrine is accessible from:  
* {{ill|East Japan Railway Company|en|East Japan Railway Company|qid=Q499071|lt=East Japan Railway Company|1=East Japan Railway Company}}, {{ill|Tokyo Metro|en|Tokyo Metro|qid=Q682894|lt=Tokyo Metro|1=Tokyo Metro}}, or [[:en:Tōyō Rapid Railway|Tōyō Rapid Railway]] {{ill|Nishi-Funabashi Station|en|Nishi-Funabashi Station|qid=Q128803|lt=Nishi-Funabashi Station|1=Nishi-Funabashi Station}}  
* {{ill|Keisei Electric Railway|en|Keisei Electric Railway|qid=Q83538|lt=Keisei Electric Railway|1=Keisei Electric Railway}} {{ill|Keisei-Nishifuna Station|en|Keisei-Nishifuna Station|qid=Q6383831|lt=Keisei-Nishifuna Station|1=Keisei-Nishifuna Station}}
